steps.title: Identify what directly supports the conclusion
steps.content: Martinez and colleagues concluded that M. barbatus associates T. caesius alarm calls with danger. The experiment only showed a reaction; to support the conclusion, we need evidence that the reaction is specific to alarm calls — not triggered by any sound whatsoever.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Other bird species than *M. barbatus* also showed a tendency to freeze in place or scatter into vegetation when Martinez and colleagues played *T. caesius* alarm calls.: broadens the finding to other species but says nothing about whether M. barbatus specifically links alarm calls to danger vs. any loud noise. ✗
  • Choice B — Martinez and colleagues played alarm calls from different *T. caesius* individuals and observed no significant variation in the responses of *M. barbatus*.: shows consistency of the response but not specificity — M. barbatus might still react the same way to random noise. ✗
  • Choice C — In some instances, *M. barbatus* froze in place or scattered into vegetation when Martinez and colleagues approached but before they began playing sounds.: suggests the birds react to human approach alone — this undermines the conclusion by introducing a confounding trigger. ✗
  • Choice D — When Martinez and colleagues played control sounds of random noise in the vicinity of *M. barbatus*, the birds displayed no reaction.: proves the reaction is specific to alarm calls, not to sound in general — directly supporting the conclusion that the birds associate alarm calls with danger. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
